name: Château de Lacypierre
address: Château de Lacypierre, Saint-Crépin-et-Carlucet, 24590 Dordogne, France
architectural_style: Layered Périgord manor-château — 15th-century defensive towers and curtain walls built on 13th-century stronghold foundations, with 16th-17th century residential additions in the Périgord Noir manner: local limestone, characteristically steep slate roofs (the regional building tradition that gives the Périgord Noir its name alongside its oak forests), and modest proportions appropriate to provincial nobility; the physical fabric reflects the layering of three separate construction phases across two centuries, with the earliest surviving stonework belonging to the post-Hundred-Years-War reconstruction and the latest to the consolidation of the house in the 17th century

opening_hours.seasonal_note: Owner-guided tours operate by appointment or via scheduled GetYourGuide slots (t1275801) — not a walk-in attraction. Tours are conducted in French only by the owners Isabelle and Florence Lebon-Hénault; check GYG for current dates and availability. The château is generally open from May through October; closed November through April. Naming note: GYG's own location tag reads 'Château de Cipières' — this is a tagging error. Every independent local and regional source (the commune, Dordogne Périgord Tourisme, Visit Dordogne Valley) confirms the property's correct name is Château de Lacypierre. Ignore the Cipières reference entirely.

best_season: May to October — the garden is at its best in late spring and early summer; the guided tour gives equal access regardless of season but the outdoor approach and gardens require good weather to appreciate fully
accessibility: The tour route includes the formal garden (flat, broadly accessible), the living rooms (ground floor, accessible), and the slate-roofed attic (stairs required). The attic section is not accessible for visitors with limited mobility; the garden and living room sections can be completed on their own with prior arrangement.
tagline: A family still gives the tour — the daughters of the couple who spent 47 years restoring this Périgord château guide you through the house where they grew up
highlights: Owner-guided by the family who restored it — Isabelle and Florence Lebon-Hénault, daughters of the couple who bought and restored the château between 1968 and 2015, give all tours themselves; the narration combines the four-century documentary history of the Bénié de Lacypierre family with childhood memories of growing up in a château being rebuilt around them

Guillaume Bénié de Lacypierre's counter-revolutionary trajectory (1792) — Guillaume (1734–1826) served as a royal bodyguard to both Louis XV and Louis XVI at Versailles; when the Revolution collapsed the world he served, he emigrated in 1792 to Koblenz, the principal assembly point of the French émigré nobility, and enlisted in the counter-revolutionary Army of Condé under the Prince of Condé — a direct personal connection between this small Périgord manor and the defining political crisis of 18th-century France

47 years of family restoration (1968–2015) — Annick and Serge Lebon-Hénault bought a largely ruined property in 1968 and spent their adult lives restoring it; their research was published as the book *Lacypierre, la mémoire retrouvée* (2018), which traces the Bénié de Lacypierre family history from 1462 to Guillaume's death in 1826

The tour route — garden, slate-roofed attic, family living rooms, antique earthenware collection — covers spaces accumulated and restored over 50 years of occupation; each room reflects both the house's documented history and the specific choices the Lebon-Hénault family made in restoring it

The post-Hundred-Years-War foundation date (1462) — the Bénié de Lacypierre family acquired the property as the Hundred Years' War drew to its final close; the château they built and expanded over the following three centuries was a product of the slow return to provincial normality in a Dordogne recently returned to French sovereignty after a century of English occupation
description:
Most châteaux in the Périgord Noir are managed by heritage trusts, municipal authorities, or hospitality groups; Lacypierre is managed by the family who restored it. Isabelle and Florence Lebon-Hénault guide visitors through a house that was their childhood, explaining rooms their parents spent half a century bringing back to life alongside a four-century history that neither they nor the house can entirely separate. The experience of visiting Lacypierre is specifically that of a property still inhabited and still personally meaningful to the people who preserved it — which makes it unlike virtually any of its famous Dordogne neighbors.

The château stands in the hills of the Périgord Noir, approximately a kilometer southeast of the village of Saint-Crépin-et-Carlucet, on a site that has been occupied and fortified since at least the 13th century. The earliest structure here was a fortified stronghold belonging to the seigneury of Salignac — Château de Salignac, visible on its ridge roughly six kilometers to the northeast, was the administrative center of the region, and the Lacypierre site was held by the Ferrières family as vassals of Salignac's lords. The landscape around Saint-Crépin-et-Carlucet is quintessential Périgord Noir: dense oak woodland on limestone plateaus, with the valleys cut by small rivers between which the manor houses and defensive towers of medieval Périgord are distributed across the high ground.

The year 1462 marks the first significant documented transition in the property's ownership. The Hundred Years' War had formally concluded nine years earlier — the French expulsion of English forces from Gascony was completed at the Battle of Castillon in 1453 — and the Dordogne was in the slow process of returning to normal provincial life after a century of intermittent occupation, raid, and depopulation. In 1462, as the Ferrières family's tenure ended, the property passed to the Bénié de Lacypierre family. That name, and the particular character of the house it would generate, would persist for over three hundred years.

The physical character of the château reflects the layering of its construction history. The earliest surviving fabric includes defensive elements from the 15th-century construction — thick walls, the rhythm of the tower positions, the steep slate rooflines — that speak to a period when even provincial manors required credible defensive capability in a landscape still recovering from a century of intermittent warfare. The 16th and 17th-century additions that constitute the main residential block are built in the Périgord tradition: local limestone, the characteristically steep slate roofs of the region, and proportions appropriate to the rural nobility of the area rather than to the grander aspirations of the Loire or the Île-de-France.

The most historically remarkable figure connected to the house is Guillaume Bénié de Lacypierre, born in 1734 and died in 1826 at the age of ninety-one. Guillaume served as a royal bodyguard at Versailles to both Louis XV and Louis XVI — a position that brought him physically close to the court of the ancien régime during the decades in which that world was visibly beginning to fracture. When the Revolution arrived in 1789 and royal authority collapsed around him, Guillaume made the choice that tens of thousands of French nobles faced: to remain in a France hostile to the class he represented, or to leave. In 1792, Guillaume emigrated to Koblenz — the German city on the Rhine that had become the principal assembly point for the French émigré nobility — and enlisted in the counter-revolutionary army organized under the Prince of Condé. The Army of Condé was a royalist force formed from French officers and nobles who refused to accept the Revolutionary government; it fought on the margins of the larger European wars against France for more than a decade before being definitively disbanded.

Guillaume returned to France under the Napoleonic amnesty for émigrés, the last Bénié de Lacypierre to carry the family's history in full. He died in 1826 without legitimate heirs, and the château's subsequent century was one of progressive decline and eventual abandonment — a fate shared by many provincial French noble properties that had lost the family structures maintaining them through the upheavals of the Revolution and its aftermath.

The restoration story begins in 1968, when Annick and Serge Lebon-Hénault bought the largely ruined property. What followed was forty-seven years of systematic physical restoration combined with equally systematic archival research into the house's history. Annick's research into the Bénié de Lacypierre family — their documented history from the 1462 acquisition through Guillaume's death in 1826, and the specific story of Guillaume's path from Versailles to Koblenz to the Condé army and back — was eventually published as *Lacypierre, la mémoire retrouvée* (2018), a book that reconstructs the family's four-century presence at the property from documentary sources. The restoration itself was completed in 2015.

Today the château is guided by Isabelle and Florence Lebon-Hénault, who grew up in the house during its restoration and now lead all visits themselves. The tour covers the formal garden (restored as part of the broader project), the slate-roofed attic (where the original structural timbers of the medieval portions of the house are exposed), the family living rooms (furnished with a combination of original pieces and objects accumulated during the restoration period), and an antique earthenware collection assembled by the family over decades of local acquisition in the Périgord markets. The daughters speak about the house in two registers simultaneously: the documentary history of the Ferrières, the Bénié de Lacypierre family, and Guillaume's particular trajectory, and the more personal account of what it meant to grow up surrounded by a house being rebuilt — a combination that is specific to this kind of owner-guided property and cannot be replicated elsewhere.

For the broader Périgord Noir castle context: [Château de Beynac](/castles/france/chateau-de-beynac) and [Château de Castelnaud](/castles/france/chateau-de-castelnaud), both in the Dordogne valley 18–22 kilometres southwest, are the principal Hundred Years' War fortifications of the region; [Château de Salignac](/castles/france/chateau-de-salignac), visible on its ridge 6 kilometers northeast, was the seigneury to which Lacypierre's earliest lords were vassals.
history: 13th century: Fortified stronghold established on the site, held by the Ferrières family as vassals of the seigneury of Salignac. 15th–17th century: Main construction phases of the current château, with defensive and residential elements added in successive periods. 1462: Property acquired by the Bénié de Lacypierre family at the close of the Hundred Years' War; the family gives the château its current name. 1734: Guillaume Bénié de Lacypierre born; later becomes a royal bodyguard to Louis XV and Louis XVI at Versailles. 1789–1792: The French Revolution collapses the world Guillaume served; in 1792 he emigrates to Koblenz and joins the counter-revolutionary Army of Condé under the Prince of Condé. 1826: Guillaume dies without legitimate heirs at age 91, the last of the Bénié de Lacypierre line. 19th–early 20th century: The château declines and falls into partial ruin. 1968: Annick and Serge Lebon-Hénault purchase the château and begin restoration. 1968–2015: 47 years of systematic restoration combined with archival research into the house and family history. 2018: *Lacypierre, la mémoire retrouvée* published, documenting the Bénié de Lacypierre family history based on Annick Lebon-Hénault's research. Present day: Owner-guided tours run by daughters Isabelle and Florence Lebon-Hénault.
how_to_visit:
**GYG guided tour (~$14, t1275801, new listing):** The standard and recommended way to visit — book via GetYourGuide for available slots and dates. Tours are given in French only by Isabelle and Florence Lebon-Hénault and last approximately 1.5 hours. Check current availability directly on GYG.

**Getting there:** Saint-Crépin-et-Carlucet is approximately 15km northeast of Sarlat-la-Canéda. No regular public transport to the château; a car is required.

**Combine with:** Château de Salignac (6km northeast), Château de Beynac and Château de Castelnaud (18–22km southwest in the Dordogne valley).

faqs.question: Why does GetYourGuide show this château as 'Château de Cipières'?
faqs.answer: This is a tagging error by GetYourGuide — possibly a confusion with another property — that has no basis in any local, regional, or historical source. The château's correct name in every independent source (the commune's own publications, Dordogne Périgord Tourisme, Visit Dordogne Valley, the family's own book *Lacypierre, la mémoire retrouvée*) is Château de Lacypierre. The property has no connection to Cipières (a village in Alpes-Maritimes). Ignore the GYG location tag entirely.

faqs.question: Are tours available in English?
faqs.answer: At time of writing, owner-guided tours at Château de Lacypierre are conducted in French only. Confirm current language availability with the GYG listing (t1275801) before booking if a French-language tour would present a difficulty.

faqs.question: Who was Guillaume Bénié de Lacypierre and why does he matter?
faqs.answer: Guillaume (1734–1826) was the last of the Bénié de Lacypierre line and the most historically documented member of the family. He served as a royal bodyguard at Versailles under both Louis XV and Louis XVI, placing him personally at the heart of the ancien régime in its final decades. After the Revolution, he was one of thousands of French nobles who emigrated rather than accept the new order — joining the counter-revolutionary Army of Condé at Koblenz in 1792. He returned to France under Napoleon's émigré amnesty, and the house's owners have researched his documented story in detail. Their book, *Lacypierre, la mémoire retrouvée* (2018), puts his trajectory from Versailles bodyguard to émigré soldier at the centre of the château's four-century history.

hero_image.alt: Château de Lacypierre — the family-restored Périgord Noir manor château at Saint-Crépin-et-Carlucet, Dordogne, France, with its characteristic steep slate roofs and local limestone walls

meta_title: Château de Lacypierre, Dordogne: Owner-Guided Visit in the Périgord Noir
meta_description: Visit Château de Lacypierre — a family-restored Périgord manor where the owners' daughters give every tour. French-guided visit from $14. Home of a royal bodyguard turned Revolutionary émigré.

gyg_featured_tours.note: New activity — no reviews yet. Tour is given in French only by the owners (Isabelle and Florence Lebon-Hénault). GYG's location tag incorrectly reads 'Château de Cipières' — this is a tagging error; the property's correct name is Château de Lacypierre.
